perturbation.original_text: 7.3 In case of a breach of the Agreement that is capable of being cured, the non-breaching party shall, before terminating the Agreement, give the breaching party written notice of such breach, and a thirty (30) day period in which to cure such breach.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.3 In case of a breach of the Agreement that is capable of being cured, the non-breaching party shall, before terminating the Agreement, give the breaching party written notice of such breach, and a ten (10) day period in which to cure such breach.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period to 10 days could be problematic under general contract law principles that require a reasonable opportunity to cure. New Jersey courts often consider industry standards and the complexity of the breach when determining reasonableness. A 10-day period may be deemed unreasonably short for certain types of breaches, creating an unenforceable clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Jersey Contract Law - Reasonable Opportunity to Cure
perturbation.law_citation: New Jersey contract law, see e.g., Dunkin' Donuts of America, Inc. v. Middletown Donut Corp., 100 N.J. 166, 183 (1985)
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts established contract law principles requiring a reasonable opportunity to cure a breach before termination. While New Jersey law doesn't prescribe a specific cure period, reducing it to 10 days may be deemed unreasonable by courts, especially for complex breaches. Dunkin' Donuts of America, Inc. v. Middletown Donut Corp., 100 N.J. 166, 183 (1985) is one of many cases discussing materiality of the breach and reasonableness, so a shorter period is suspect.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the cure period from 30 days to 10 days. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both indicate that this could be deemed an unreasonable amount of time to cure a breach, thereby contradicting the general contract law principle requiring a reasonable opportunity to cure a breach before termination. The provided materials suggest that New Jersey courts assess reasonableness based on the specific circumstances of the breach.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 If the Company elects to create and market the Products, the Company will pay to the Professional a "Royalty Fee" on the sales of Products during the Term, except as provided in the following sentence, of two percent (2%) of the "Royalty Base," which Royalty Base shall be calculated as the wholesale selling price of all Products for which the Company actually receives the proceeds of such net of returns, allowances, discounts, shipping, taxes, insurance and credits. During the Term, the Company shall pay the Royalty Fee, earned for the preceding quarter, to the Professional quarterly, within thirty (30) days of the end of the succeeding calendar year quarter.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 If the Company elects to create and market the Products, the Company will pay to the Professional a "Royalty Fee" on the sales of Products during the Term, except as provided in the following sentence, of two percent (2%) of the "Royalty Base," which Royalty Base shall be calculated as the wholesale selling price of all Products for which the Company actually receives the proceeds of such net of returns, allowances, discounts, shipping, taxes, insurance and credits. During the Term, the Company shall pay the Royalty Fee, earned for the preceding quarter, to the Professional annually, within one hundred eighty (180) days of the end of the calendar year.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the royalty payment from quarterly within 30 days of the succeeding quarter to annually within 180 days of the end of the year could violate wage payment laws if the professional is considered an employee in New Jersey. Even as an independent contractor, unreasonably delaying payment could be seen as a breach of contract and good faith.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Jersey Wage Payment Law - N.J.S.A. 34:11-4.2
perturbation.law_explanation: N.J.S.A. 34:11-4.2 mandates timely wage payments. While this section explicitly covers "wages" and might not directly apply to royalty fees for an independent contractor, unreasonably delaying these payments (to 180 days annually) could be construed as a violation of good faith and fair dealing, or be reclassified as wages, especially if the 'independent contractor' status is challenged. The original quarterly payment structure is more in line with typical wage payment schedules.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces an inconsistency regarding the payment timeline, potentially violating wage payment laws. The original text specified quarterly payments, which aligns better with typical wage schedules. The changed text delays payment to annually, potentially violating good faith or being reclassified as wages and therefore violating wage laws. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both suggest this delay could be problematic under N.J.S.A. 34:11-4.2 if the professional is considered an employee or if the delay is deemed unreasonable.

perturbation.original_text: 8.1 The Company may renew this Agreement on the same terms and conditions for one (1) additional five year period that shall begin on January 1, 2005 and end on December 31, 2009, by providing a written notice of its intent to effect such renewal to the Professional by November 30, 2004.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.1 The Company may renew this Agreement on the same terms and conditions for one (1) additional five year period that shall begin on January 1, 2005 and end on December 31, 2009, by providing a written notice of its intent to effect such renewal to the Professional by December 30, 2004.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notification date to December 30, 2004, makes the renewal ineffective. In contract law, timely notice is essential for exercising options like renewal. Giving notice almost a full month after the deadline renders the option invalid, creating a dispute regarding the contract's continuation. It is a clear breach.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Basic Contract Law - Option Contract Principles (Timely Exercise)
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification creates a clear breach of contract. According to Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 25, an option contract requires strict adherence to the terms for exercise. Providing notice of renewal *after* the specified deadline (November 30, 2004, changed to Dec 30, 2004) renders the renewal ineffective. The Company fails to properly exercise its option, and the contract expires, potentially leading to legal disputes and damages.

perturbation.justification: The modification, by altering the notification date from November 30, 2004, to December 30, 2004, introduces a clear breach of contract. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation confirm that this makes the renewal ineffective due to the failure to adhere to the original deadline. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' where a time-sensitive obligation (renewal notification) does not align with the original contractual requirements. This makes the modified terms unenforceable.
